reasoning: On May 29, 2025, the market estimated an 18% probability of an Israeli strike on Iranian nuclear facilities before July. The primary drivers for this were reports of US intelligence assessing that Israel was "preparing" to quickly strike if US-Iran negotiations fail, coupled with a 2-month deadline set by President Trump in March. As of June 8, negotiations have not formally collapsed; the fifth round on May 23 ended with "no agreement but possibility of progress," and Gulf states are lobbying Washington to prevent a strike. However, the IAEA Board of Governors is scheduled to meet June 9-13, where the US and European allies are drafting an accusatory resolution against Iran. If passed, this could theoretically trigger an escalation or collapse of talks, opening a window for Israeli action in late June. Over a third of the active time window (~41 days from May 20 to June 30) has already elapsed without kinetic action, pushing the probability down over time. Furthermore, historical base rates strongly indicate that Israeli full-scale kinetic strikes on actual nuclear installations are exceedingly rare despite constant threats (as opposed to striking air defenses or military bases nearby, which would likely not resolve this market). Given the time decay, diplomatic hesitations, and the high threshold required to directly strike a nuclear site, the current probability is assessed at 12%.
